subject: [PATCH v7 0/3] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

reviews.author: Christian Brauner
reviews.summary: Christian Brauner expressed strong disagreement with AI-Assisted-by tags, considering them useless noise in the git log and suggested that they should be removed when applying patches, while advocating for crediting actual contributors.
reviews.raw_body:
Picking up on something we discussed at LSFMM in one of the sessions as
an aside rant: I find these AI Assisted-by tags so useless tbh and just
pure noise in the git log _especially_ for a core developer like Jeff
that I really don't see the point of them and I'm always tempted to just
remove the tags when I apply. I have dropped them before because I found
them so pointless.

Crediting Jan here is the right thing to do and it provides actual value
and also just makes sure that a real person who spent time helping out
gets visibility in the git history. Why we should extend the same
courtesy to automated tooling is really beyond me. Somehow we've become
all convinced that these tools require a special status but have spent
months arguing about the usefulness of other tags.

reviews.author: Christian Brauner
reviews.summary:
The reviewer pointed out a redundant check in the code, noting that test_and_set_bit() returns the old value of the bit, so the subsequent test_bit() call is unnecessary and can be removed.

Reviewer Christian Brauner noted that the wb_get() call is unnecessary because unlocked_inode_to_wb_begin() already holds a reference to wb, and requested that it be removed.

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary: The author acknowledges that adding Assisted-by tags is a matter of fairness, but expresses uncertainty about their usefulness and whether they should be added at all.
reviews.raw_body:
To be clear, Christoph and Ritesh also contributed a lot of review and
suggestions.

I was mainly trying to follow this new verbiage in
Documentation/process/submitting-patches.rst:

------------------8<-------------------
Using Assisted-by:
------------------
If you used any sort of advanced coding tool in the creation of your patch,
you need to acknowledge that use by adding an Assisted-by tag.  Failure to
do so may impede the acceptance of your work.  Please see
Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst for details regarding the
acknowledgment of coding assistants.
------------------8<-------------------

If we're demanding this from anyone, then we should demand it from
everyone. I don't think we want one set of rules for core contributors
and another set for other folks.

As to whether we should add them at all -- I don't know. I think it
really comes down to what we intend to do with this info. I'll play
devil's advocate for the moment:

The cost of adding these tags is low. It's just a few extra bits in the
repo. Maybe this could eventually have historical value?
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

reviews.author: David (Arm)
reviews.summary: Reviewer David expressed concern that the patch description lacks a detailed explanation of how AI tooling was used, making it unclear whether the changes are trustworthy.
sentiment_signals: expressed skepticism about unchecked AI output
reviews.raw_body:
As we don't really know "how" AI tooling helped. it's pretty useless without a
more detailed description I'm afraid.

OTOH, we want people (in particular not trusted community members) to indicate
that it might all just be unchecked AI output.

Hm.

-- 
Cheers,

David

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary: Author is asking Christian Brauner if they would like him to re-send the patch with a specific change, indicating no further revision planned for this particular issue.
reviews.raw_body:
That does look much cleaner. Do you want to just make that change or
would you rather I resend?

Thanks!

2026-05-11.patch_summary: This patch series improves write performance by addressing issues related to RWF_DONTCACHE, a flag that prevents caching of dirty pages. The patches fix integer promotion bugs on 32-bit systems, ensure proper stat accounting during cgroup writeback domain migration and folio split operations, and prevent use-after-free (UAF) errors in filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback(). Additionally, the patches track DONTCACHE dirty pages per bdi_writeback, have the flusher write back a proportional number of pages after a DONTCACHE write, and include new benchmarks to test competing buffered and dontcache writers.

reviews.author: Jan Kara
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that they are not familiar with the code but the patch looks good, and explicitly requested to be added as Reviewed-by
reviews.raw_body:
I'm not really very knowledgeable in this code but the change looks good to
me. Feel free to add:

Reviewed-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>

								Honza

reviews.author: Jan Kara
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch series improves write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE, but did not raise any specific technical issues or concerns.
reviews.raw_body:
Looks good. Feel free to add:

Reviewed-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>

								Honza

reviews.author: Jan Kara
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch, with Christian's simplification, addresses all issues and is ready for inclusion; he explicitly states it 'looks good' and requests to be added as a Reviewed-by.
reviews.raw_body:
This patch with Christian's simplification looks good. Feel free to add:

Reviewed-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>

								Honza

2026-05-12.patch_summary: This patch series improves write performance by addressing issues related to RWF_DONTCACHE, a flag that prevents caching of dirty pages. The patches fix integer promotion bugs on 32-bit systems, ensure proper stat accounting during cgroup writeback domain migration and folio split operations, and prevent use-after-free (UAF) errors in filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback(). Additionally, the patches track DONTCACHE dirty pages per bdi_writeback, have the flusher write back a proportional number of pages after a DONTCACHE write, and include new benchmarks to test competing buffered and dontcache writers.

reviews.author: Ritesh (IBM)
reviews.summary: The reviewer noted that collect_wb_stats() does not collect DONTCACHE stats, which could be useful for debugging purposes, and suggested adding this functionality in a separate patch if needed.
reviews.raw_body:
Using wb_stat infra was a clever thing to do for counting the number of
dontcache folios.
I see that we don't collect DONTCACHE stats in collect_wb_stats(), which
I guess, is mainly for debug purposes only. Either ways I am not sure
how useful that might be, since it only shows the approximate stats
since it doesn't do wb_stat_sum() for most of the other stats too.
If for any reason we need that in future, that could go in a separate patch.

For this patch - LGTM. Please feel free to add:
Reviewed-by: Ritesh Harjani (IBM) <ritesh.list@gmail.com>

reviews.author: Ritesh (IBM)
reviews.summary: The reviewer noted that the patch improves write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE, and after considering multiple requests for performance numbers, it looks good to them. They requested to add a Reviewed-by tag.
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks for also considering multiple request for performance numbers
into account, this indeed is a nice improvement overall to
RWF_DONTCACHE.

With Christian simplification - the patch looks good to me.
So, please feel free to add:

Reviewed-by: Ritesh Harjani (IBM) <ritesh.list@gmail.com>

2026-05-13.patch_summary: This patch series improves write performance by addressing issues related to RWF_DONTCACHE, a flag that prevents caching of dirty pages. The patches fix integer promotion bugs on 32-bit systems, ensure proper stat accounting during cgroup writeback domain migration and folio split operations, and prevent use-after-free (UAF) errors in filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback(). Additionally, the patches track DONTCACHE dirty pages per bdi_writeback, have the flusher write back a proportional number of pages after a DONTCACHE write, and include new benchmarks to test competing buffered and dontcache writers.
